descriptors.name: Roasty
descriptors.hint: Dry, espresso-like character from dark malts. Think dark bread crust or coffee grounds.

descriptors.name: Burnt
descriptors.hint: Acrid, carbon-like — more extreme than roasty. Can indicate over-roasted malt or a process flaw.

descriptors.name: Dark Fruit
descriptors.hint: Plum, prune, fig — typically derived from heavily kilned malts or fermentation at high temperatures.

descriptors.name: Dried Fruit
descriptors.hint: Raisin, prune, fig — common in oxidized beers or strong dark ales like barleywines and old ales.

descriptors.name: Rich
descriptors.hint: Deep, full, concentrated malt presence — like malt extract or wort. Not a flaw, but can be excessive.

descriptors.name: Smoky
descriptors.hint: Wood smoke character — from smoked malts (Rauchbier) or smoked water. Intentional in Bamberg-style lagers; may be a flaw in other styles.

descriptors.name: Citrus
descriptors.hint: Lemon, orange, grapefruit — bright and zesty. Common in American and New World hop varieties.

descriptors.name: Herbal
descriptors.hint: Fresh or dried herbs like rosemary, oregano, thyme, or mint. Typical of noble and traditional European hops.

descriptors.name: Floral
descriptors.hint: Rose, lavender, jasmine — delicate and perfume-like. Often found in lager and English ale hops.

descriptors.name: Resinous
descriptors.hint: Pine sap, cannabis — from high-alpha hops like Chinook, Simcoe, or Columbus.

descriptors.name: Spicy
descriptors.hint: Black pepper, clove, or peppery bite from hops. Common in noble varieties like Saaz and Hallertau.

descriptors.name: Tropical
descriptors.hint: Mango, pineapple, guava, maracuyá — characteristic of New World hops like Citra, Galaxy, or Mosaic.

descriptors.name: Earthy
descriptors.hint: Damp soil, roots, cellar — that forest-after-a-rainstorm aroma. Can be a hop character (English varieties) or light Brett influence.

descriptors.name: Woody
descriptors.hint: Raw wood, cellar, sawdust. Different from oak — less structured, more rough.

descriptors.name: Grassy
descriptors.hint: Fresh-cut grass or green, vegetal hop character. Common with fresh/wet hops or early harvest varieties.

descriptors.name: Green
descriptors.hint: Raw, green hop character from freshly harvested or undried hops — similar to grassy but more vegetal and unripe.

descriptors.name: Banana
descriptors.hint: Isoamyl acetate — a classic Hefeweizen ester. A flaw in lagers or IPAs; desirable in wheat beers.

descriptors.name: Stone Fruit
descriptors.hint: Peach, apricot, nectarine, plum — typical of ale yeast esters at moderate fermentation temperatures.

descriptors.name: Clove
descriptors.hint: 4-vinylguaiacol — a phenol desirable in Hefeweizen and Witbier. A flaw in most other styles.

descriptors.name: Medicinal
descriptors.hint: Band-aid, antiseptic, TCP — chlorophenol from wild yeast (S. diastaticus) or chlorinated rinse water. Always a flaw.

descriptors.name: Solvent / Fusel
descriptors.hint: Acetone, nail polish remover, or sharp hot alcohol — fusel alcohols from stressed or over-heated yeast. Always a flaw.

descriptors.name: Syrup
descriptors.hint: Heavy, sticky sweetness — can indicate incomplete fermentation or very high residual sugar.

descriptors.name: Citric
descriptors.hint: Bright, lemon-like acidity. Common in wheat beers and some sours. Lighter and crisper than lactic.

descriptors.name: Sour / Acidic
descriptors.hint: General sourness from organic acids. Can be lactic (smooth, yogurt-like) or acetic (sharp, vinegar-like). Assess whether appropriate for the style.

descriptors.name: Lactic
descriptors.hint: Smooth, yogurt-like sourness from Lactobacillus. Desirable in Berliner Weisse, Gose, and kettle sours.

descriptors.name: Brettanomyces
descriptors.hint: Wild yeast producing leather, barnyard, earthy, and funky notes. Prized in Belgian and wild ales; a flaw in clean styles.

descriptors.name: Oak
descriptors.hint: Vanilla, coconut, toasted wood tannins — from barrel aging. Can add complexity or dominate if over-oaked.

descriptors.name: Oxidized
descriptors.hint: Wet cardboard, paper, sherry-like — from oxygen exposure post-fermentation. Always a flaw in fresh beers.

descriptors.name: Sulfur
descriptors.hint: Struck match, gunpowder, or rotten egg (H₂S) — from lager yeasts or stress. Often dissipates with lagering or gentle swirling.

descriptors.name: Vegetal
descriptors.hint: Cooked vegetables, cabbage, broccoli — DMS-related. A flaw in nearly all styles.

descriptors.name: Musty
descriptors.hint: Damp cellar, mold, old cork — from mold contamination or poorly sanitized equipment and storage.

descriptors.name: Yeasty
descriptors.hint: Bread dough or autolysis (meaty, savory) — from excess yeast in suspension or yeast breakdown during aging.

descriptors.name: Diacetyl
descriptors.hint: Butter or butterscotch — from incomplete fermentation or Pediococcus contamination. A flaw in most styles except some English ales.

descriptors.name: Acetaldehyde
descriptors.hint: Green apple, cucumber — an intermediate compound from incomplete fermentation. Indicates immature or under-attenuated beer.

descriptors.name: DMS
descriptors.hint: Dimethyl sulfide — cooked corn, canned vegetables, vegetable broth. From insufficient boil or slow wort chilling. Always a flaw.

descriptors.name: Estery
descriptors.hint: Overly fruity, ester-driven character beyond style expectations — from high fermentation temperatures or certain yeast strains.

descriptors.name: Light-Struck
descriptors.hint: Skunky, sulfur-like — from UV or fluorescent light reacting with iso-alpha acids. Worse in clear or green bottles.

descriptors.name: Lacing
descriptors.hint: Foam rings left on the glass as you drink — a sign of good protein and hop content.

descriptors.name: Legs
descriptors.hint: Droplets running down the glass after swirling — indicates high alcohol or significant residual sugar.

descriptors.name: Particles in Suspension
descriptors.hint: Visible particles — often live yeast in bottle-conditioned or unfiltered beers. Desirable in some styles.

descriptors.name: Lingering
descriptors.hint: Bitterness or flavor that persists long after swallowing. Can be a positive quality or a sign of excess.

descriptors.name: Cloying
descriptors.hint: Heavy, sticky sweetness that coats the mouth and does not invite another sip. Indicates incomplete attenuation.

descriptors.name: Metallic
descriptors.hint: Coins, blood, iron — from rusty equipment, high-iron water, or hop compounds reacting with metal surfaces.

descriptors.name: Butter
descriptors.hint: Butterscotch — diacetyl from incomplete fermentation or Pediococcus contamination. A flaw in most styles.

descriptors.name: Cooked Corn
descriptors.hint: DMS (dimethyl sulfide) in flavor — canned corn, vegetable broth. From insufficient boil or slow wort chilling.

descriptors.name: Green Apple
descriptors.hint: Acetaldehyde — an intermediate fermentation compound. Indicates immature beer or incomplete fermentation.

descriptors.name: Gushing
descriptors.hint: Beer foams uncontrollably when opened — from over-carbonation, infection, or nucleation sites in the liquid.

descriptors.name: Hot / Burning
descriptors.hint: Burning sensation in the mouth or throat from excessive fusel alcohols or carbonation.

descriptors.name: Slick
descriptors.hint: Oily or slippery texture — can indicate yeast autolysis, infection, or excessive diacetyl.

scoring_guide.description: Major off flavors and aromas dominate
Off flavors/aromas or major style deficiencies
Misses the mark on style and/or moderate flaws
Generally within style parameters, minor flaws
Exemplifies style well, requires minor fine-tuning

steps.title: 1. Aroma
steps.hint: Sniff immediately after pouring, while volatiles are most intense. Evaluate malt, hops, fermentation character, and any off-aromas. Gentle swirl to release more compounds.

steps.title: 2. Appearance
steps.hint: Assess color, clarity, and head. Use good lighting against a white background when possible.

steps.title: 3. Flavor
steps.hint: Take a moderate sip and let it coat the palate. Evaluate malt character, hop flavor and bitterness, fermentation byproducts, balance, finish/aftertaste, and any off-flavors.

steps.title: 4. Mouthfeel
steps.hint: Note body, carbonation level, warmth (alcohol), creaminess, and astringency.

steps.title: 5. Overall Impression
steps.hint: A holistic judgment: how well the beer fits its declared style, drinkability, overall enjoyment, and constructive feedback.

sections.label: Appearance
tasting_hints: Use proper lighting. Hold the glass up to the light or against a white background to assess the true color.
Tilt and look through the edges — it may reveal reddish or copper hues.
Haze can be intentional (NEIPA, Hefeweizen) or a flaw depending on style.

aspects.label: Color
aspects.tasting_hint: SRM (Standard Reference Method) measures light absorption. Color doesn't always predict flavor — a stout can be smooth, a golden beer very bitter.

aspects.label: Clarity
aspects.tasting_hint: Brilliant means perfectly filtered. Hazy can be intentional (NEIPA, Hefeweizen) or indicate contamination. Opaque is normal in some unfiltered wheat beers.

aspects.label: Head Color
aspects.tasting_hint: Darker malts produce darker foam. Very beige foam on a pale lager can indicate lipid contamination.

aspects.label: Head Formation
aspects.tasting_hint: Head is affected by temperature, CO2 level, and pour technique. A glass with detergent residue will destroy head formation.

aspects.label: Head Retention
aspects.tasting_hint: Time how long the head lasts after pouring: Poor = under 15s, Moderate = 15–60s, Good = over 60s. Malt proteins and iso-alpha acids from hops are the main contributors.

aspects.label: Other
aspects.tasting_hint: Lacing on the glass indicates good protein quality. Legs suggest high alcohol or residual sugar. Particles may be live yeast — desirable in some styles.

sections.label: Aroma & Flavor
tasting_hints: Take short sniffs right after pouring — volatile aromas are most intense early.
First sip: assess temperature and carbonation. Second sip: let the beer coat your entire palate.
Look for how aromas and flavors connect: attack, mid-palate development, and finish.
Gently chew the beer to release CO2 and reveal more flavors.

aspects.label: Malt
aspects.tasting_hint: Evaluate both aroma and flavor: grain, bread, caramel, and dark malt notes. Malt sweetness should balance bitterness in the mid-palate.

aspects.label: Hops
aspects.tasting_hint: New World hops give citrus and tropical notes; traditional European hops are herbal, floral, and spicy. Hop flavor (from dry hopping, late additions) is separate from bitterness.

aspects.label: Fermentation Character
aspects.tasting_hint: Esters (fruity) and phenols (spicy/earthy) from yeast. A strong banana in a lager is a flaw; clove in a Hefeweizen is expected.

aspects.label: Off-Flavors / Flaws
aspects.tasting_hint: Diacetyl = butter. DMS = cooked corn. Acetaldehyde = green apple. Oxidized = wet cardboard. Medicinal/plastic is always a flaw. Sulfur may dissipate with swirling.

aspects.label: Sweetness
aspects.tasting_hint: Perceived at the tip of the tongue. Excessive sweetness in a dry style indicates incomplete attenuation.

aspects.label: Bitterness
aspects.tasting_hint: Felt on the palate and in the finish. Harsh or astringent bitterness can indicate excessive late hopping, incorrect water chemistry, or tannin extraction.

aspects.label: Acidity
aspects.tasting_hint: Lactic (smooth, rounded) is desirable in Gose, Berliner Weisse, and sours. Acetic (vinegar-like) is sharper, felt in the throat — a flaw in most styles.

aspects.label: Balance
aspects.tasting_hint: Balance doesn't mean everything should be in the middle — an IPA should be bitter, a Märzen malty. Evaluate whether the balance fits the style.

aspects.label: Finish / Aftertaste
aspects.tasting_hint: The finish is what lingers after swallowing. A dry, clean finish invites another sip. Assess whether it is appropriate for the style.

sections.label: Mouthfeel
tasting_hints: Mouthfeel is evaluated alongside flavor — focus on texture rather than taste.
Notice how the beer feels at the beginning, during, and after swallowing.
Astringency is that dry, rough feeling, like biting into an unripe fruit skin.

aspects.label: Body
aspects.tasting_hint: The weight and viscosity you feel as beer coats your mouth. Think skim milk (thin) vs. whole milk (medium) vs. cream (full/heavy). Residual sugars, proteins, and dextrins build body. A thin stout or a heavy lager are style faults.

aspects.label: Carbonation
aspects.tasting_hint: Bubbles should be fine and persistent — a sign of quality. Large, quickly dissipating bubbles often indicate aggressive forced carbonation.

aspects.label: Alcohol Warmth
aspects.tasting_hint: A slight warmth is desirable in high-ABV beers. If it's burning or solvent-like, the yeast fermented at too high a temperature.

aspects.label: Creaminess
aspects.tasting_hint: The texture of the beer as it moves across the palate — silky and coating in creamy beers, sharp and fleeting in watery ones. Distinct from body (weight); this is purely surface texture. Oats, wheat, and nitrogen all produce creaminess.

aspects.label: Astringency
aspects.tasting_hint: Astringency feels like rough dryness on gums and cheeks. It can come from tannins due to over-milling, high-temperature sparging, or excessive hopping.

aspects.label: Flaws
aspects.tasting_hint: A flat beer (no CO2) or gushing beer (too much gas) are process or service flaws. Slick or oily texture can indicate yeast autolysis or infection.

sections.label: Overall Impression
tasting_hints: Step back from individual aspects: do you want another glass?
Think about the complete experience — do all the elements work together?
If evaluating against style, consider whether this beer would represent that style well.

Consider the 4 main fault categories: Infected (wild yeast, bacteria), Fermentation off-flavors (diacetyl, acetaldehyde, fusels), Oxidized (cardboard, sherry, stale), Style deviations (wrong bitterness, color, body, aroma profile).

aspects.label: Style Accuracy
aspects.tasting_hint: Does it meet the style parameters? Could someone identify the style without being told? Consult the BJCP style guidelines for exact parameters.

aspects.label: Technical Merit
aspects.tasting_hint: Is it well-crafted? Look for absence of flaws, clean fermentation, clarity (where applicable), correct head. A beer can be a minor style but impeccably made.

aspects.label: Drinkability
aspects.tasting_hint: Do you want to keep drinking it? Would you share it with someone? A beer can be stylistically correct but fatiguing. Drinkability is key to the overall experience.

defects_guide.label: Diacetyl
defects_guide.cause: Incomplete fermentation, yeast that did not reabsorb diacetyl, Pediococcus contamination
defects_guide.style_exception: Acceptable at very low levels in some English ales and Czech lagers

defects_guide.label: DMS (Dimethyl Sulfide)
defects_guide.perception: Cooked corn, canned vegetables, vegetable broth
defects_guide.cause: Insufficient boil, slow wort chilling, bacterial contamination
defects_guide.style_exception: Very low level acceptable in some American lagers

defects_guide.label: Acetaldehyde
defects_guide.perception: Green apple, cucumber, freshly cut grass
defects_guide.cause: Incomplete fermentation, immature yeast, insufficient conditioning

defects_guide.label: Oxidation
defects_guide.perception: Wet cardboard, paper, sherry, stale apple
defects_guide.cause: Post-fermentation oxygen exposure, high storage temperature, poorly sealed bottle
defects_guide.style_exception: Desirable in barrel-aged beers, Old Ale, aged Barleywine

defects_guide.label: Astringency
defects_guide.perception: Rough dryness, unripe fruit skin, tannin
defects_guide.cause: Over-milling, excessively hot sparging, excessive hopping, prolonged mash

defects_guide.label: Medicinal / Plastic Phenol
defects_guide.cause: Wild yeast (Saccharomyces diastaticus), chloramines in rinse water
defects_guide.style_exception: None (distinct from the desirable spicy phenol in Hefeweizen)

defects_guide.label: Sulfur
defects_guide.cause: Certain yeast strains (lager), yeast stress, Zymomonas contamination
defects_guide.style_exception: Can appear in fresh lagers and dissipates with lagering. Very low level acceptable.

defects_guide.label: Acetic Acid
defects_guide.perception: Vinegar, sharp sensation in the throat
defects_guide.cause: Acetobacter contamination (vinegar bacteria), oxygen exposure during fermentation
defects_guide.style_exception: Sought after and desired in Lambic, Gueuze, and some American Wild Ales

defects_guide.label: Metallic
defects_guide.cause: Rusty equipment, high-iron water, hop reaction with metals

defects_guide.label: Solvent / Fusel
defects_guide.cause: Fermentation at too high a temperature, stressed yeast, insufficient yeast nutrition

defects_guide.label: Excessive Lactic Acid
defects_guide.cause: Uncontrolled Lactobacillus contamination in styles that do not require it
defects_guide.style_exception: Desired and sought in Berliner Weisse, Gose, Lambic, and sours in general

defects_guide.label: Light-Struck (Skunky)
defects_guide.cause: Exposure to UV or fluorescent light (especially in green or clear bottles)
defects_guide.style_exception: None (though some consumers mistakenly associate it with certain imported brands)

defects_guide.label: Musty
defects_guide.perception: Damp cellar, mold, cork, old basement
defects_guide.cause: Mold contamination, infected cork, poorly sanitized equipment or storage

defects_guide.label: Yeasty
defects_guide.cause: Excessive yeast in suspension, yeast autolysis from aging on dead yeast, poor filtration
defects_guide.style_exception: Low levels acceptable in bottle-conditioned and unfiltered styles
